does: This paper presents a method for automatically generating diverse adversarial prompts that elicit harmful responses from large language models. The core contribution combines GFlowNet fine-tuning with a secondary smoothing phase to train an attacker language model to produce varied and effective attack prompts. Unlike reinforcement learning approaches that suffer from mode collapse despite diversity regularization, the GFlowNet approach is probabilistically principled and maintains a distribution over diverse attacking strategies. The method is evaluated on multiple target LLMs with and without safety tuning, demonstrating that generated attacks transfer effectively across different models and succeed in exposing vulnerabilities in existing safety mechanisms.
connects: This work extends the automated red-teaming literature that has traditionally relied on reinforcement learning to discover adversarial prompts. It responds to documented limitations of RL-based approaches including mode collapse and insufficient diversity in attack discovery, problems that have constrained the comprehensiveness of safety evaluation. The paper builds on GFlowNet theory, which has emerged as an alternative to RL for sampling from complex distributions in a more principled way. It also engages with broader research on adversarial robustness, safety tuning of LLMs, and prompt engineering, while positioning red-teaming as essential infrastructure for responsible AI deployment alongside other safety evaluation techniques.
enables: This approach enables more thorough safety evaluation of LLMs by discovering a richer set of attack vectors that existing methods might miss. By generating diverse attacks, it supports more comprehensive safety tuning—developers can use the discovered attacks to train more robust models against varied failure modes. The method also establishes GFlowNet fine-tuning as a practical alternative to RL for diversity-critical applications beyond red-teaming. Future work could leverage the diverse attacks for understanding failure mode clusters, developing targeted defenses, and establishing adversarial benchmarks. The transfer capabilities suggest potential for efficient red-teaming where diverse attacks discovered on one model can accelerate evaluation of new systems.
